name: South Carolina Dept of Social Services (DSS)
tagline: South Carolina's human-services backbone: SCDSS runs child welfare, SNAP/TANF and other public benefits, and child support — a high-volume, data-heavy agency mid-stream in both a court-monitored child-welfare reform and a multi-year benefits-mainframe modernization.
overview: The South Carolina Department of Social Services (SCDSS) is the state's primary human-services agency, responsible for child welfare (abuse/neglect intake, investigations, and foster-care case management), public benefits (SNAP, TANF, and related economic-services programs), and child support enforcement. It is one of South Carolina's most data-intensive agencies: its eSAM benefits mainframe serves more than 300,000 households and roughly 630,000 individuals and processes on the order of $1.5B in annual benefit payments, while child-welfare casework runs on CAPSS, the state's SACWIS-class case-management system. SCDSS operates under federal oversight from the long-running Michelle H. v. McMaster child-welfare settlement, which has driven a multi-year child-welfare transformation and continuing data-quality and workload requirements. For a Dell SLG seller, SCDSS is a large, modernization-driven buyer with two active technology fronts — replacing/modernizing the aging benefits mainframe and improving the integrity and tooling of child-welfare case data — reached primarily through South Carolina's central statewide IT contracts rather than agency-only RFPs.

budget.intro: SCDSS is a direct spender funded through South Carolina's annual General Appropriations Act plus large federal streams (SNAP, Title IV-E child welfare, child-support, and CCDF child care). For FY2027, Director Tony Catone presented a request of roughly $114.6M to expand child-welfare supports and child care and to continue modernizing the benefits mainframe — a proposed ask, not a final enacted appropriation, so confirm the signed budget before forecasting.

stats.label: FY2027 request
stats.note: Agency ask for child welfare, child care, prevention, and benefits-system modernization; not yet enacted

stats.label: Benefits-mainframe match
stats.note: Nonrecurring state matching funds requested for the multiyear eSAM modernization; ~50% expected federal share

stats.label: Benefit payments
stats.note: Annual benefit volume processed through the eSAM mainframe (300K+ households)

notes: SCDSS funding mixes state general revenue with large federal match (SNAP/TANF administration, Title IV-E, CCDF, child support) — IT and modernization spend often rides federal cost-allocation rules, splitting project cost roughly half state / half federal.
The governor's executive budget separately included $25M in recurring funds for a child-welfare salary plan; staffing and Michelle H. workload requirements are recurring cost drivers.
Figures cited are the agency's requested numbers as presented to legislative subcommittees — confirm the enacted FY2027 appropriation before committing to a forecast.

initiatives.name: eSAM benefits-mainframe modernization
initiatives.summary: SCDSS is pursuing a multiyear replacement/modernization of the aging eSAM mainframe that determines SNAP/TANF eligibility for 300K+ households and processes ~$1.5B in annual payments. The agency has requested successive installments (most recently $18.6M nonrecurring state match) and expects a new system to come online over the next couple of years — a long runway for cloud, data, integration, and migration work.

initiatives.name: Child-welfare transformation & CAPSS data integrity
initiatives.summary: Under the Michelle H. settlement and court monitors, SCDSS is reforming foster care and improving the quality and integrity of data in CAPSS, its SACWIS case-management system. Documentation gaps and data-entry integrity have been flagged publicly — a surface for case-management tooling, data quality, integration, and analytics.

initiatives.name: Contact-center & benefits citizen experience
initiatives.summary: SCDSS modernized 19 contact centers and its IVR with Amazon Connect and added real-time SNAP status notifications and video quality-control interviews; SNAP application timeliness rose from ~84% to 95%+ in 2025. Reliability, capacity, and digital experience remain priorities — an infrastructure and benefits-experience play.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & data protection
initiatives.summary: SCDSS holds highly sensitive child-welfare, benefits, and child-support data and shares it across agencies and with federal partners. Statewide security sits under the SC Dept of Administration's Division of Information Security (SCDIS), whose SCDIS-200/210 standards apply — data protection, identity, and compliance are persistent requirements alongside both modernization tracks.

procurement.intro: Selling to SCDSS runs through South Carolina's centralized procurement framework under the Consolidated Procurement Code, administered by the Office of State Procurement (OSP) / Division of Procurement Services. Confirm the right path early: most commodity IT flows through statewide term contracts (with a dedicated IT term-contracting team), while larger or specialized programs — like the eSAM modernization or child-welfare systems — run as their own competitive agency solicitations, sometimes via sole-source determinations.

vehicles.name: Statewide Term Contracts (IT)
vehicles.detail: OSP / Division of Procurement Services establishes statewide term contracts for IT goods and services usable by all governmental bodies — the default path for routine SCDSS hardware, software, and services. Searchable at procurement.sc.gov/contracts.

vehicles.name: Vendor registration (SCBO / OSP)
vehicles.detail: Vendors must be registered to do business with the state and monitor opportunities through South Carolina Business Opportunities (SCBO); confirm Dell's registration and statewide-contract coverage before pursuing deals.

vehicles.name: Agency / program solicitations
vehicles.detail: Large or specialized programs — notably the eSAM benefits modernization and child-welfare (CAPSS) systems — run as their own SCDSS competitive solicitations or sole-source determinations; confirm the buying entity and vehicle before pursuing.

vehicles.name: Cooperative contracts
vehicles.detail: South Carolina governmental bodies may also use authorized cooperative vehicles (e.g., NASPO ValuePoint) for some IT acquisitions — an alternative route onto SCDSS opportunities.

powerStructure.name: Tony Catone
powerStructure.title: State Director, SCDSS
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Gov. McMaster (acting Jan 2025, appointed Mar 2025) and Senate-confirmed in May 2025; former agency general counsel. Sets agency direction and presents the budget — the top relationship for the SCDSS account.

powerStructure.name: Don Grant
powerStructure.title: Deputy State Director
powerStructure.note: Senior operational leadership across the agency — a key cross-program decision-maker.

powerStructure.name: Jose Encarnacion
powerStructure.title: Information Technology Director
powerStructure.note: Owns SCDSS systems including the eSAM benefits platform and CAPSS — the core technology buying center for modernization, data, and infrastructure work.

powerStructure.name: Amber Gillum
powerStructure.title: Deputy State Director, Economic Services
powerStructure.note: Owns SNAP/TANF and benefits-eligibility programs — the buying center behind the eSAM modernization and benefits citizen-experience work.

powerStructure.name: Dawn Barton
powerStructure.title: Deputy State Director, Child Welfare Services
powerStructure.note: Leads foster care and child-welfare reform under Michelle H. — the buying center for CAPSS case-management and child-welfare data needs. (CFO Susan Roben owns finance.)

news.title: SC joins federal 'A Home for Every Child' foster-care initiative to recruit and retain foster homes
news.impact: Renewed child-welfare push under Catone; sustained demand for CAPSS case-management data, foster-home tracking, and reporting tied to placement goals — though the announcement itself is program, not IT.

news.title: SCDSS blocks out-of-state and online EBT transactions to curb SNAP skimming/fraud (effective Apr 28, 2026)
news.impact: Benefits fraud-prevention and payment-integrity controls (card-lock via ConnectEBT portal/app/IVR) are now an active front — a data-protection, identity, and transaction-security surface alongside the eSAM modernization.

news.title: New federal SNAP work requirements (OBBBA) take effect; ABAWD age band expands to 18–64, exemptions narrowed
news.impact: Expanded work-requirement tracking and eligibility redeterminations raise workload and rules-engine complexity for the eSAM benefits system — a driver for the mainframe-modernization and integration roadmap.

news.title: Director Catone presents ~$114.6M FY2027 request: child welfare, child care, and benefits-mainframe modernization
news.impact: Signals continued investment in modernization and child-welfare reform, including another installment for the eSAM mainframe — confirm the enacted appropriation before forecasting.

news.title: SCDSS launches video calling for SNAP quality-control interviews to meet new federal requirement
news.impact: Continued benefits-experience modernization on cloud/contact-center infrastructure — recurring demand for reliable capacity and integration.

news.title: SC Senate confirms Tony Catone as SCDSS Director
news.impact: Stable, permanent leadership at the top of the account — re-confirm priorities for the term and anchor the executive relationship.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's coverage on South Carolina statewide IT term contracts (OSP / Division of Procurement Services) and active vendor registration/SCBO monitoring — the required access path for routine SCDSS IT buys.

Lead with the eSAM benefits-mainframe modernization: position cloud-foundation infrastructure, data platform, storage, and migration/managed services around the multiyear, federally cost-shared rebuild.
Engage the child-welfare transformation (Michelle H. / CAPSS) with data-integrity, integration, and analytics infrastructure that supports court-monitored reporting and casework quality.

Tie benefits citizen-experience and contact-center capacity to compute, storage, and infrastructure at high transaction volume, building on the existing AWS contact-center footprint rather than displacing it.

Anchor relationships with Director Catone, IT Director Jose Encarnacion, and the Economic Services and Child Welfare deputies, and map statewide security/compliance under the SC Dept of Administration's Division of Information Security (SCDIS) before forecasting security and data deals.
